speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number three for Edith Danko, August 6, 1995. OK, you finished the last tape by telling me that there-- there was still lots more to tell about Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: About Auschwitz. That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, so tell us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we was staying there in-- in blocks, and sleeping of-- was three, like three floors. And five girls of one-- one bed. And if one fall down from the top, the whole-- all of us was down. And, you know, broken everything. Bleeding. And it was happen. So you often [INAUDIBLE] what else. Then, every morning, they took us out [INAUDIBLE] to counting if we are all here. One day, it was missing two girls. They was lying someplace, dead. They was thinking that they run away. They kept us a whole day, outside. And we got to kneel. This was one Jewish woman-- I mean, we was all a Jewish there. She didn't want to kneel. She said, a Jewish woman shouldn't kneel. And they beat her so-- I mean, we was there. Nobody moved. They beat her to death. But she didn't want to kneel. She cried. She said, I shouldn't kneel. It was very bad this time. And then they find the two girls, dead. They just was going with a wagon and picking up, every morning, the dead people, and throw them over the wagon, like-- like in a-- like piece of wood. Everybody was skinny, and just half dead, [? with ?] us. And this was twice a day, what they took us, and they count us, if everybody is there. OK, after it happened, we got a Jewish girl. She was the [NON-ENGLISH]. She helped to put us-- I mean, when they, the German, the SS, come in to count, to us that we stay straight and everything. Then her sister, with husband, was some place hiding. They catch them. They brought them. And they-- then her sister-- they put in the same barrack, [INAUDIBLE] us. She was pregnant. They didn't know this. She was in such a dress that they didn't know that she is pregnant. She got the baby in barrack. And the SS woman-- she passed by there. And she heard the baby cry. And she came in. She took the baby. And she stepped one feet, with her boots. With the other, she pulled. And she torn the baby to pieces. And that's all of that. [CRYING] And they killed her, right away-- the mother. And they killed the baby-- both of them. [INAUDIBLE] we was there in Auschwitz [? not ?] long time. Then they took the-- one day, they-- the [NON-ENGLISH], the sister, from the woman what they got the baby there, she knew that we had the last transport to going out from Auschwitz. This was already in '44, I think. She knew that we are going the last out from there. And I was lying, already, between the dead people. Well, I was so sick with diarrhea. I couldn't-- I couldn't move. I couldn't do nothing. And she know I'm still alive. She took-- went there, and pulled me out, where I was lying. She talked to me. And I-- I don't know if I know what she's saying, other than she begged me. Please, stay just how long they were counting. Well, that's it. What go-- get out from Auschwitz. She beat me on both side. And she did like this to me, and of the hand, just-- just the-- that I feel in [? a ?] to staying how long they will count us. OK, I was stand there, and everything. They count us. And they left-- the Germans. And they put us in another barrack-- and not all of the girls. When we was there, it was about-- I don't know, 200, [? other ?] how much what they send us out, to where-- to where they keep us a few days. And they gave us a little more to eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we got every day just [INAUDIBLE] radish soup. [INAUDIBLE] radish soup. In there, they give us something a little more to eat. Then we can-- and we can go out to work. And they took us to [? Boizenburg. ?] They said this is close by Hamburg-- Hamburg. They took us there. And other blocks-- they put us in. And then, in the morning, they took us to work. It was cold, winter. They get us wooden shoes, the Holland shoes-- no socks, no nothing, no underwear, no-- just the black dress on you. With this, we went to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the factory, OK-- then we got a little more to eat, already. And they gave me [? a ?] [? metal. ?] It was marked, just to keep us onto the-- onto the cutter, to cut, to cut the metal. This-- this was my job. I mean, a lot of them worked this, or there [INAUDIBLE] got other things to do there, in the factory. And we worked there. And then every morning, they took us to work. In the evening, they brought us home. But one day, something a girl did there-- I don't know if she steals two potatoes, or there [INAUDIBLE]. And they gave us, for everybody, 25 of the behind, for all of us. And we couldn't sit. We couldn't do nothing. In the morning, when we came in shop, everybody from us pulled up the dress, and showed the behind, for the [NON-ENGLISH] there. He didn't said nothing. He was-- he was a nice man. He used to bring such a little sandwich, and used to put in a half for me, in the drawer. And he wouldn't say, you got something there. He just looked at me, and went away. He shouldn't do this. And he did this a few times. But it was not enough for a baby to eat, [? from ?] the sandwich, what he got [INAUDIBLE]. It's OK. He gave me a piece [INAUDIBLE] too.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so, we worked there. And everything was OK. Once, I got [? from ?] a tooth-- I was swollen, swollen [? to ?] this. I didn't complain to nobody. When she count us, then she saw my face was swollen. She asked me, what is this? Did somebody hurt me? I said, no. And we didn't talk, even, between the girls, between us. We were scared to talk. We was so quiet, so like-- like I said, like if you've seen the and people move [INAUDIBLE]. And that's what I felt.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, in the morning, I went back to work. I came home in the evening. She prepared a dentist. She got the ready in camp, when we was in Boizenburg, a dentist. And she came to me. And she said, lay down on the floor. And open your mouth. He pulled out four mine-- four of the five tooths mine. They was all healthy, good tooths. Nothing was-- their dentist was a young boy from Holland. He cried. Such a tears was falling from his eyes. I screamed. And she just kicked me with a boot, where she can. And then, it wouldn't be alarm. She would ask him all the tooths to pull out, just with the ones come in the alarm. Then we have to running in, and to turn off the light, if not every--

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind-- you mean that there were planes coming?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm?

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of alarm?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. It was security around. And they make the alarm other-- maybe they just make the first alarm. A lot of the time, when they beat us, they used to scream [INAUDIBLE], leave them alone, in German, and to the SS girls. The woman was worse, like the man. They was terrible. The woman-- this was something. Worse like the man. And they-- they used, a lot of the times, to scream [INAUDIBLE] when they watched us. What they doing-- even when they beat us, they give us the 25 for everybody, of the behind. Then they scream [INAUDIBLE]. Leave them alone. [INAUDIBLE] it wouldn't be the alarm, then he would-- then all of my teeths would be out from the mouth. Yes, and she kicked [INAUDIBLE] OK. Went to work. And home the next time, then what happened. I cut off my finger. And she took to me, in the night there-- well, this was in the night we worked.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you didn't cut off your finger. Oh, you did.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It's the boss was stand by the-- by me. Well, he was-- he had to watch us. If he wouldn't stop the machine, then would go on, like this. Just he stopped, right away, the machine. We were-- not, I will say, [INAUDIBLE]. And we was so weak. We were so sleepy. We didn't got nothing. We just didn't know even nothing. And then she took me there, in-- like here, but in a clinic, [INAUDIBLE] in an ambulance, middle of the night. And the old man, what he was so nice and everything. He picked up the piece. And he left. The blood was falling. But yet-- I don't know, three stitches, two stitches. He was a young man, what he come back from the-- he was in army. He came back. And he saw this blood. He start to cry. And he cried. And their old man put-- picked up this-- the other piece, and left. Then she came with me there, in the ambul-- in the clinic. Was there Germans, womans. But they-- they were, sitting-- man and woman, with children, and doctor. And they saw how I was look, and how this bleeding. And they said to her that she should go in with me before them. And she said, the SS woman, no, she have to wait when her line will be, when her turn will come. And then she have-- she have to wait. Then another told her, you take her in right away. She wouldn't. She was sitting with me, a long-- I was the last to get-- I mean, was behind me, you know-- last to get in. Then I didn't went more to work. She took me back in the factory. And I was sitting there, how long all the finishing. And then she took me home. And I was staying in the barracks. The girls was going to work. And I was working clinic again, by the SS-- the rooms. And I was washing. And after this, I was in the kitchen, helping in the kitchen. [SIGHS] [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] I can't-- I don't know if-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] then I was-- I was staying-- I [? was ?] cleaning by the [? man. ?] Then a woman was-- then the SS woman met me. She was watching me, that I don't talk, they shouldn't talk to us, or that-- or that I don't steal nothing. She was such a [? this-- ?] I was standing of my knees, and scrubbing the floor. And she was [INAUDIBLE] make laugh. They didn't look nothing. I mean, if this would happen today, it wouldn't be nothing by [INAUDIBLE]. This time, I just felt like animals, or that they kept us for animals.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And after this, I was working in the kitchen. And I used to take the peel-- the [INAUDIBLE] the potatoes, what I used to peel. The peels-- this I used to bring in the barrack, and give the girls to eat. They was even not washed. Well, I couldn't-- didn't got [? water ?] to washing them. And if they would see that I take them, they would beat me up. And I just bring them inside. And they ate, like-- like they was something else. I couldn't-- I couldn't bring even-- take for me nothing, but one bite. [? Because ?] then we was there how long it's come to the end of the war. It's come to the end. And when I'm going out from [INAUDIBLE] and work already. We was there [NON-ENGLISH]. He was [? new boss. ?] He said he'd come just from America. And then he know from which side the American are coming, and from which side the Russian. And he wanted to take us to the American side. You know, a lot that they killed in the last minute, or that he wanted to be a nice man, and come and shown that he has helped us. We survived. And we survived because of him. And then, in the middle of the night, we walked out from there, from the camps, and all of the SS man, with everybody with us. And we was walking night and days-- dark, just in a forest. They woman, they right away undressed. They put on dresses. They took out the-- the SS. And they was dressed up like men, in a-- in SS clothes. They took out. And the minute, we didn't recognize them. They was already in the dresses, dressed up nice. They got already the-- brought the, you know, the machine guns what they got with them. [? Throwing ?] away. Some [? throwed ?] away. Some cried and break them off on his knee. And then I don't know how many days we was in there, in forest, going like this. And one night, he went to a-- to a German family. And he said he needed I don't know-- so much bread. And he said, in a few hours, you have to deliver me the bread. And he brought, and gave us, for everybody, a piece of bread. And then, in the morning, some right in the morning, I saw the per-- they picked up the hand. And they went. And they left us. And they all picked up the hand and went to-- they saw the American soldiers there. And then, you know, they took them. They took them first. And then they came. They took us. And they give us to eat. And-- and that's what-- that's how I-- we was free, already.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were liberated by American soldiers.

speaker: interviewee
text: By American.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the date?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was May, I think, 5th, or the 4th, or the 6th-- the 6th of May. Something like this. You know, it was hard. And we didn't got paper. And we didn't talk to one with the other. Rad-- radio, I mean, there, or there's something. We didn't know the days. And we didn't know nights. We didn't know nothing. And we didn't talk, even between us. But we just sit there. We couldn't-- nothing. We was just like dope-- for doped. That's what we looked, all. And then the American took us, [? all right. ?] They give us something-- the white bread, for everybody, to eat bread. And we was there a few weeks. And then I find that, with the one cousin, and we was together, in the same barrack-- everything. She was much younger like I. Is-- and we-- then we decided we wanted to go on back home, after-- and there, where we wanted to go, on the Russian bus. The American could not take in there. They said we have to go on the Russian side. Then we went of the Russian side. They lef-- they lost-- they left us. They said, if you want to go, fine. You go ahead. And then they will take you home. And we came on the Russian side. They put us in-- in another camp. And they gave us work, by horses. They said, the trains are not moving. The cars is not moving. The roads are broken. You cannot go on home. And we was there maybe about two months, or there-- more. More-- I mean, it's already four. And we are still in camp, in the camps. Here and we've just finished. And here we are again, in the camps. And we sleep there, on the-- by the train stations.

speaker: interviewer
text: What camp did they put you in then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't-- I don't know where this was, or if this was in Germany. I don't know which place exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was liberated. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we start, between us, to talking. We was three-- three cousins. And I was there. And one cousin of ours, the youngest, got a boyfriend. And he said, I will take and-- well, the Russian wouldn't let us go. And he said, the middle of the night, I will take two of you. And I will go on through, over the border. And then I will come on back. And I will take the other two. And that's what he did. And then we went back on the American side. When we came over the American side, we was in Munchen. People was, already, in Romanian, Hungarian, and Czechoslovakian-- they're already back. And it's-- it's already, you know-- it's already about three, four months, like we [INAUDIBLE] liberated. And we just stand there, and we don't-- didn't know what is going on. And then we was in Munchen. In Munchen, they sent us to Fernwald. In Fernwald, we was there, all that-- four together, in one room, three cousins and I. They live, all three, in Cleveland, Ohio. And-- and then, we was there. And-- and I met my husband. One of-- one of the cousins met my husband. And she brought them in house. And she know I'm older than her. And she got a sister older. She didn't think to marry. And just she find a man, she just brought him in, like. And then, this-- when my husband came in, [INAUDIBLE] we knew each other for 11-- 11 weeks. And we married. And even the first night, we didn't got where to go in to sleep. We [INAUDIBLE] married there. Well, he was staying with boys. And I was staying with [INAUDIBLE] the cousins. Then my cousin, the oldest, just said, let them sleep here. She said, let them sleep of the bed. Just let them be here. And then, it took us a few days. Then we moved in, in Fernwald. In the house, there was one couple, and two men. And then we moved in with them. And my husband start to work. And he worked already in hospital, there in Fernwald. And he brought, you know, such a-- to covering up a corner, from the hospital. And that's what he put this. And we was-- and we lived there, with them, together. [INAUDIBLE] we was staying there. And we didn't got where to go on. And I just told my husband, [? where ?] we'll be the first registration. I wanted gone out from here. And I still remembered the-- from my brother, how he said, if you survive, go to Palestine. Still it was Palestine this time. [INAUDIBLE] we didn't got where to go. And nobody was ready to take us in. And we was sitting in Fernwald. And then we was free. Then was mine daughter was born in there. And I start just to complain. And I don't want to be here. I couldn't stand-- I couldn't stand the Germans that used to coming in, and this, and this, and the German doctors to go on. I just-- I couldn't take it this more. I couldn't-- I didn't want it more to be in Germany. Then was-- the first registration was to Norway. And we went there [INAUDIBLE], I didn't feel there too. My husband always said-- I said that my daughter will not marry a Jewish boy. It was-- it was not this. I told them this. I was thinking. And I looked for every person that dies. I was thinking, maybe I know it's my family, so-- [CRYING] And then it was, when we went to Norway, the Norwegian was nice to us. I am not saying not even they didn't was nice, and they was-- and they were-- when it was the war, when the Jews--

speaker: interviewer
text: [PHONE RINGING] Shit.

speaker: interviewee
text: Go in the bedroom. [PHONE RINGING] Is-- [PHONE RINGING]

speaker: interviewer
text: Maybe I should go and pick up the phone. No, it's all right. He'll take care of it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Is-- when we came to Norway, it was nice. They put us in a school a few weeks that we can go on. And my husband got after this a job. And I was then pregnant with my son. He was born there. He was born in Norway. And everything was very, very [INAUDIBLE]. Just I couldn't be there. I just feel to go on. And on this, they start already with Israel. And we knew what is going on. This will be our Israel, maybe. And-- and I said to my husband, if it will be a registration, we will go on to Israel. He didn't want to go on. He was scared to go on there. I mean, I don't blame them. We went from the one this, and go on another one this-- that is [? more. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: As we came-- OK, we was there. My son is born there. And exactly they made peace then. You know? They-- we didn't know exactly when they will proclaim that it's Israel. And I was just, wish-- I wish my son was born the same day. But other-- no, he didn't come the same day. Then I was thinking, in the 17th of May is the-- is the [INAUDIBLE] of Norway. I said maybe-- maybe he will born on this day. No, he picked his own-- he picked him own day, when he came.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we was there. And then we went to Israel. And [INAUDIBLE] we got there enough. We got very hard, hard times there. Nothing. We didn't got nothing. A little water even-- no. We have to stay and wait for a drink of water, and with the diapers. First, when we came in, we stayed in the street, with the two children. We didn't got where to go on. I mean, they-- they was ready to make some room for us, to take us some place. So there was too many-- too much people used to coming in. And they couldn't do this. Although still I was feeling so good now I'm in Israel. Because was already Israel. I feel-- even when I was lying outside with the two children, and they gave us, for everybody, an orange-- I think something to drink, too. It was hot. And Norway was very cold weather. And that I still, inside in me, I feel so good I am there. We didn't got nothing-- nothing much, like the children, and my husband. And I--

speaker: interviewer
text: But you felt better being in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. I don't know why. Well, maybe I remembered my brother, what he told me-- go to Palestine. But just to be in Israel. And there, I start to looking for every man and woman [INAUDIBLE]. I was thinking, I will find somebody. I just was going to the walls, where was notes. There was notes that's-- the names from the people, if the one was looking for the other one. It was hot there. But I still, inside was in me happy. My husband was [? crying ?] there, and I--

speaker: interviewer
text: He hated it there?

speaker: interviewee
text: It-- it was very hard. It was hard. And we were sitting, four people in a [NON-ENGLISH], just in a [NON-ENGLISH], like they call it there. You got to wait for a drink of water. You have to wait-- if-- if you will have the milk, if you have the quarter for the little milk, you didn't were sure that the milk woman will come with the milk. If she has milk, we'll have milk, even. It was very hard-- hard, from beginning. It's hard-- I mean, it's today, it's hard, too--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: --although it's different-- different things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you both work in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't work. I didn't work. I didn't work to here, to in American-- in America.

speaker: interviewer
text: But he did?

speaker: interviewee
text: He worked. He was always. He worked-- he worked very hard. He went to [INAUDIBLE]. He worked by a [? building. ?] [NON-ENGLISH], they called this, [NON-ENGLISH]. He was there 24 hours. And I didn't know where he disappeared. He couldn't-- they make the roof. And they couldn't leave this in the middle. They got to finish in this. There was no telephone to call home where he is. And I don't know what happened. And he was working there. And then he came home after, I don't know, maybe 24 hours. And I didn't know where he disappeared, what happened to him. And we didn't got telephone. We didn't got nothing. Because it was-- you got to stay and wait for a bus. You got to stand on the [NON-ENGLISH], walking around. It was very hard. And then, after 10 years, we came back here.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, so let's stop right now.
